originalTitle: Trump exploring additional tariffs on Canada for wildfire smoke impacts in US, White House official says – US politics live
summary: New tariffs announced Monday would take effect in 30 days unless Canada removes barriers on US auto, alcohol and dairy products
content: Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, has released a statement in response to threats from Donald Trump to impose new 50% tariffs on Canadian goods in 30 days unless Canada removes barriers on US auto, alcohol and dairy products. Carney noted that all of the measures imposed by Canada on imports from the US that Trump complained of on Monday were taken in retaliation to previous US tariffs imposed by Trump. Here is Carney’s full statement: “Today, the United States administration announced its intention to impose a new 50% tariff on a significant number of Canadian goods. This is the latest in a series of unilateral U.S. trade actions that began with the U.S. imposing a series of tariffs in direct violation of the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA), the free trade agreement between Canada, the United States, and Mexico. These include tariffs on the Canadian auto sector, in violation of CUSMA. Canada, as is its right, has merely matched those measures. More broadly, in response to these measures and threats to Canadian sovereignty, provinces, territories, and Canadians from coast to coast to coast have stood together, taking the necessary actions to support our economy and defend our workers, farmers, businesses, and families. Recognising that the U.S. has been transforming all of its trade relationships, including those covered under CUSMA, over the past 18 months, Canada has made a series of detailed and comprehensive proposals to resolve this dispute and to modernise CUSMA. We stand ready to intensify those discussions in the coming weeks. Canada believes in the benefits of free and fair trade, as evidenced by our new government signing more than 20 new economic and security partnerships. This trade dispute has raised costs for families, particularly in the U.S. Canada stands ready to engage intensively to address outstanding issues with the U.S. to the mutual benefit of our citizens. In all circumstances, Canada will work relentlessly and take any measures necessary to build our strength at home and to support Canadian workers, farmers, businesses, and families.” As part of the Trump administration’s continuing attack on left-leaning groups, the US state department has listed several activists and organizations as “front groups and fellow travelers” for Cuba, including Hasan Piker and Amazon Labor Union founder Chris Smalls. In a 100-page report released on Monday, the state department denounces Cuba as the “capital of 21st-century communism”, accusing the Cuban government of carrying out a “campaign of subversion” that has “infiltrated the highest reaches of the US government, recruited and cultivated generations of American activists [and] backed an unprecedented wave of leftwing terrorism on American soil”. The state department report alleges that the so-called campaign is “designed to turn America against itself” and singles out several left-leaning activists and organizations, including the Democratic Socialists of America, Isra Hirsi – the daughter of Minnesota Democratic representative Ilhan Omar – New York City mayor Zohran Mamdani, and journalist Amy Goodman, citing their criticism of US policy toward Cuba or participation in Cuba solidarity movements. Describing the DSA, of which Mamdani is a member, the report says the organization “serves as a particularly potent illustration of the ideological victory of Cuba’s effort to position itself as the spiritual capital of Third Worldist radicalism”. It accuses the DSA of maintaining a “fierce, almost religious commitment to the cause of the Cuban regime – not because the group is controlled by Cuban agents, per se, but because that commitment is now simply the de facto policy orthodoxy on the American far left”. Trump has doubled down on similar messaging in recent days, using the term communism at political rallies and presidential engagements alike. For more than 60 years, the US has maintained a sweeping economic embargo on Cuba, in addition to crippling sanctions imposed by numerous administrations. Despite US claims that its policies are designed to pressure the Cuban government, Havana and many international critics including human rights organizations say they have severely hurt the island’s economy. American distillers are worried that Donald Trump’s new 50% tariffs on spirits imported from Canada, announced on Monday and due to go into effect 19 August, could intensify the trade war the president began last year, denting their profits. “For nearly a year and a half, American spirits have been pulled from store shelves across much of Canada as collateral damage in a broader trade dispute unrelated to our sector, and we appreciate the Administration’s recognition of the significant damage these restrictions have caused U.S. distillers,” Chris Swonger, chief executive of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States, said in a statement on Monday. “We had hoped, however, that this issue could be resolved without further escalation,” Swonger continued. The new tariff “deepens trade tensions and raises the risk of further retaliation at a time when many U.S. hospitality businesses continue to face financial hardships,” he added, before calling for “policymakers on both sides of the border to pursue a negotiated solution”. The US trade group makes clear what the Trump administration has not: that Canadians only stopped selling US alcohol last year in response to Trump’s actions. “Canadian provinces removed U.S. spirits from retail shelves in March 2025 in retaliation to U.S. tariffs on Canadian goods,” the council says. As a result, US exports to Canada “fell more than 70% year-over-year from the start of the retaliatory ban in March 2025 through December 2025,” the industry group adds. The bans have only been lifted in two Canadian provinces: Alberta and Saskatchewan. The president of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, Candace Laing, called for talks between the US and Canada before Donald Trump’s newly announc
chineseTitle: 白宫官员表示，特朗普正在考虑对加拿大征收额外关税，以应对野火烟雾对美国的影响 - 美国政治直播
chineseSummary: 周一宣布的新关税将于 30 天内生效，除非加拿大取消对美国汽车、酒类和乳制品的壁垒
chineseContent: 加拿大总理马克·卡尼发表声明，回应唐纳德·特朗普的威胁，除非加拿大取消对美国汽车、酒类和乳制品的壁垒，否则加拿大将在 30 天内对加拿大商品征收新的 50% 关税。卡尼指出，特朗普周一抱怨的加拿大对美国进口产品采取的所有措施都是为了报复特朗普此前对美国征收的关税。卡尼的声明全文如下：“今天，美国政府宣布打算对大量加拿大商品征收新的 50% 关税。这是美国一系列单边贸易行动中的最新一起，这些行动始于美国征收一系列关税，直接违反了加拿大、美国和墨西哥之间的自由贸易协定《加拿大-美国-墨西哥协定》(CUSMA)。其中包括对加拿大汽车行业征收关税，违反了《加拿大-美国-墨西哥协定》(CUSMA)。 CUSMA：更广泛地说，为了应对这些措施和对加拿大主权的威胁，各省、地区和全国各地的加拿大人都站在一起，采取必要的行动来支持我们的经济并保护我们的工人、农民、企业和家庭。认识到美国在过去 18 个月里一直在改变其所有贸易关系，包括 CUSMA 所涵盖的贸易关系，加拿大提出了一系列详细而全面的建议来解决这一问题。我们准备在未来几周加强这些讨论，我们的新政府签署了 20 多个新的经济和安全伙伴关系，这证明了这一贸易争端增加了家庭的成本，特别是在家庭方面。
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
